walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Ingenious set up. First, unfold the game board and place it in the center of the table. The board is covered in hexagonal spaces arranged in a big hexagon shape. You will notice the spaces come in different shades. With 2 players, you only use the white spaces in the center. With 3 players, you also include the light grey spaces around the edge. With 4 players, you use the entire board, including the dark outer ring. Make sure everyone knows which spaces are in play for your player count.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Tile Bag
walkthrough.text: Now grab the cloth bag and all 120 tiles. Each tile is a domino-shaped piece made of two connected hexagons, and each hexagon shows one of six color symbols: circles, stars, diamonds, crosses, suns, and hexagons. Drop all the tiles into the bag, give it a good shake to mix them up, and set the bag where everyone can reach it. The bag is your draw pile for the entire game.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Score Boards
walkthrough.text: Each player takes one score board and places it in front of them. The score board has six colored tracks, one for each symbol color in the game. Each track runs from 0 to 18. This is where you will track how well you are doing in each of the six colors. Your goal is to push all six tracks forward, not just one or two.

walkthrough.title: Place Your Scoring Pegs
walkthrough.text: Each player takes six colored pegs, one matching each color symbol on the score board. Place each peg at the starting position, which is the 0 space to the right of each color symbol. All six pegs start at zero. During the game, you will slide these pegs forward along the tracks as you score points in each color.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Your Tile Rack
walkthrough.text: Each player takes one tile rack and places it in front of their score board. The rack works like a Scrabble rack. It holds your tiles upright so only you can see them. The score board acts as a natural screen to keep your tiles hidden from other players. Make sure your rack is angled so nobody across the table can peek at your tiles.

walkthrough.title: Draw Your Starting Hand
walkthrough.text: Each player now reaches into the bag and draws 6 tiles at random. Place them on your rack so only you can see the color symbols. Take a moment to look at what you have drawn. You will see pairs of color symbols on each tile. Some tiles might have two of the same color, and some will have two different colors. These six tiles are your opening hand.

walkthrough.title: Note the Printed Symbols on the Board
walkthrough.text: Before you start playing, look at the board. You will see six color symbols already printed directly onto the board, one of each color, arranged around the center. These printed symbols are important for the very first round. Each player's first tile must be placed adjacent to a different one of these six printed symbols. After the first round, you can place tiles on any two connected empty spaces.

walkthrough.title: Determine the First Player
walkthrough.text: The youngest player goes first, and play proceeds clockwise around the table. That is it for setup. The board is out, scores are at zero, everyone has six tiles in hand, and you are ready to start placing tiles and scoring points. Let's learn how to play.

2 players: white spaces only; 3 players: add light grey; 4 players: entire board

bullets: Place all 120 tiles into the cloth bag
Shake well to randomize — this is the draw pile for the whole game

Six colored tracks from 0 to 18, one per symbol color

summary.title: Place Your Scoring Pegs
bullets: Each player takes 6 colored pegs
Place each peg at value 0 on the matching color track

summary.title: Set Up Your Tile Rack
bullets: Each player takes 1 tile rack
Place in front of score board — keeps tiles hidden from other players

summary.title: Draw Your Starting Hand
bullets: Draw 6 random tiles from the bag
Place on your rack so only you can see them

summary.title: Note the Printed Symbols on the Board
bullets: 6 color symbols are pre-printed on the board near the center
First-round tiles must be placed adjacent to a different printed symbol each

summary.title: Determine the First Player
bullets: Youngest player goes first
Play proceeds clockwise

walkthrough.title: Turn Overview
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you do three things in order. First, you play one tile from your rack onto the board. Second, you score the tile you just placed. Third, you draw one new tile from the bag to refill your rack back to six tiles. That is the entire turn structure. Play a tile, score it, draw a tile. Simple and clean.

walkthrough.title: Playing a Tile
walkthrough.text: Choose one tile from your rack and place it onto any two connected empty hexagonal spaces on the board. Remember, each tile covers exactly two hexagons. The tile can be oriented in any direction as long as both hexagons land on valid empty spaces. There is one special rule for the first round: each player must place their first tile adjacent to a different one of the six pre-printed symbols on the board.

walkthrough.title: How Scoring Works
walkthrough.text: This is the heart of the game. After placing your tile, you score each of the two hexagons on it separately. Pick one hexagon to start with. That hexagon has a color symbol on it. Look at the five lines radiating outward from that hexagon, following the hex grid directions. For each line, count how many consecutive matching symbols you can see, whether printed on the board or on previously placed tiles. Stop counting when you hit an empty space or a different symbol. Do not count the symbol you just placed. Add up all matching symbols from all five lines. That total is your score for that color.

walkthrough.title: Scoring the Second Hexagon
walkthrough.text: Now do the same thing for the other hexagon on the tile you placed. It might be the same color or a different color. Count matching symbols along the five lines radiating from that second hexagon, again ignoring the symbol itself. Advance the matching color peg on your score board by the count. If both hexagons are the same color, you score that color twice, once from each hexagon's perspective.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Example
walkthrough.text: Let's say you place a tile with blue on one end and red on the other. From the blue hexagon, you see two blue symbols in one direction and one blue in another. That is 3 points for blue, so you advance your blue peg 3 spaces. From the red hexagon, you see one red in one direction only. That is 1 point for red, so advance your red peg 1 space. It is totally normal to score zero from a hexagon if there are no matching symbols adjacent in any direction.

walkthrough.title: The 18-Point Cap and Ingenious Bonus
walkthrough.text: Your scoring pegs cannot go past 18 on the track. That is the maximum for any single color. When one of your pegs reaches exactly 18, call out Ingenious and you earn a bonus play. Before drawing a new tile, immediately play another tile from your rack and score it. You can chain multiple bonus plays if scoring the bonus tile pushes another color to 18. Only after all bonus plays are done do you draw tiles to refill your rack.

walkthrough.title: Drawing a New Tile
walkthrough.text: After placing your tile and completing any bonus plays, draw one tile from the bag to refill your rack back to six tiles. If the bag is empty, you simply play without drawing. Your turn is now over and play passes to the next player clockwise.

walkthrough.title: Swapping Your Tiles
walkthrough.text: There is one special option available to you. Before you draw your replacement tile, check your rack. If none of the tiles on your rack contain the color that is currently at the lowest position on your score board, you may swap your entire hand. Show your tiles to all players to prove you have none of that color. Then put all your tiles aside, draw 6 new tiles from the bag, and return your old tiles to the bag. This is a powerful catch-up mechanism.

walkthrough.title: Game End
walkthrough.text: The game ends when no more tiles can legally be placed on the board, which usually means all playable spaces are filled. When this happens, every player checks their score board. Your final score is your lowest color peg. Not your highest, not your total, but your lowest. The player whose lowest peg is the highest wins the game.

walkthrough.title: Tiebreaking
walkthrough.text: If two or more players are tied on their lowest color, you compare the next lowest color as a tiebreaker. If still tied, keep comparing the next lowest until the tie breaks. In the extremely rare case that a player manages to push all six of their color pegs to 18, that player wins immediately, no matter what turn it is. The game stops right there.

walkthrough.title: Why the Lowest Score Matters
walkthrough.text: This is the most important rule in the game and it changes everything about how you play. Since your final score equals your lowest color, having one color at 3 and another at 18 means your score is 3. You need to push all six colors forward as evenly as possible. Ignoring any color is a losing strategy. This is what makes Ingenious such a clever game. You are constantly balancing six priorities at once.

walkthrough.title: Variant: 4-Player Partnership
walkthrough.text: As a variant, players sitting opposite each other can form teams. Both partners share a double-length score board made by placing two regular boards end to end. Each partner scores individually onto the shared board. No communication is allowed between partners during play, though you can agree to allow open communication if your group prefers. The partnership with the better lowest score wins.

summary.title: Turn Overview
bullets: Play one tile, score it, draw one tile
Three simple steps every turn

walkthrough.title: Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: Let's walk through a practice game with four players: Alex, Beth, Carlos, and Dana. Everyone has drawn their starting six tiles, all pegs are at zero, and the full board is in play. Alex is youngest, so Alex goes first. Follow along and you will have the rules down in no time.

walkthrough.title: Alex's First Tile
walkthrough.text: Alex looks at the six pre-printed symbols on the board and picks the blue circle symbol. Alex has a tile with blue on one end and green on the other. Alex places it so the blue hexagon is right next to the printed blue symbol. Now Alex scores. The blue hexagon sees the printed blue symbol in one direction, so that is 1 point for blue. The green hexagon has no green neighbors yet, so 0 points for green. Alex moves the blue peg to 1.

walkthrough.title: Beth's Opening Move
walkthrough.text: Beth places her tile next to the red printed symbol. She has a red-red double tile. She places it so one red hexagon is adjacent to the printed red. That hexagon scores 1 for red. The other red hexagon is not adjacent to any other red symbols, so it scores 0. Beth moves her red peg to 1 and draws a replacement tile.

walkthrough.title: Carlos and Dana Take Their Turns
walkthrough.text: Carlos places next to the yellow symbol, scoring 1 yellow. Dana places next to the orange symbol, scoring 1 orange. Everyone has now placed one tile and claimed a different starting symbol. The board is starting to fill in around the center. Each player draws a tile to refill to six.

walkthrough.title: Building Lines of Color
walkthrough.text: On round two, Alex places a tile with blue on one end near the blue tile already on the board, extending a line. Now this blue hexagon sees two blue symbols in one direction: the previous tile and the printed board symbol. That is 2 points for blue. Alex's blue peg moves from 1 to 3. This is how you build up big scores: extend existing lines of matching colors.

walkthrough.title: Mistake 1: Ignoring a Color
walkthrough.text: A few turns in, Beth is doing great with red and green, both at 6 points each. But she has completely ignored purple. Her purple peg is still at 0. She keeps playing tiles that score red and green because those lines are long and satisfying. This feels productive, but remember: your score equals your lowest color. Beth's score right now is 0, no matter how high red and green climb.

walkthrough.title: Why Beth's Strategy Backfires
walkthrough.text: Beth realizes her mistake when she checks her score board. Red is at 8, green is at 7, yellow is at 4, blue is at 3, orange is at 2, and purple is at 0. Her actual score? Zero. Meanwhile, Alex has all six colors between 3 and 5. Alex's score is 3, which is already better than Beth's. The lesson: a balanced approach beats a lopsided one every time.

walkthrough.title: Recovery Plan for Beth
walkthrough.text: Beth shifts her focus immediately to purple and orange, her two weakest colors. She looks for tiles with purple or orange on them and targets board positions where she can score those colors. Even scoring 2 or 3 points in a weak color is more valuable than scoring 5 more in a color that is already high. Always feed the hungry colors first.

walkthrough.title: Carlos Scores Big
walkthrough.text: Carlos places a yellow tile at the intersection of two long yellow lines. From one hexagon, he counts 3 yellow in one direction and 2 yellow in another. That is 5 points from a single hexagon. His yellow peg jumps from 4 to 9. Big scoring moments like this happen when you extend existing lines or place tiles where multiple lines converge.

walkthrough.title: The Tile Swap in Action
walkthrough.text: Dana checks her rack and realizes she has no orange tiles at all, and orange is her lowest color at 1. Before drawing her replacement tile, she shows her rack to everyone, proving there is no orange. She puts all six tiles aside, draws 6 new tiles from the bag, and returns her old tiles to the bag. She now has a fresh hand that hopefully contains some orange. The swap is not guaranteed to help, but it gives you a fighting chance.

walkthrough.title: Mistake 2: Forgetting to Score Both Hexagons
walkthrough.text: Carlos places a tile but only counts the score from one hexagon, forgetting the second one entirely. Another player catches it and reminds him. Always score both hexagons on your tile. Each tile gives you two scoring opportunities, one per hexagon. Missing one means leaving points on the table. Take your time and check all five directions from each hexagon.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game Score Check
walkthrough.text: Let's look at everyone's score boards halfway through the game. Alex: all colors between 5 and 8, lowest is 5. Beth: colors range from 3 to 10, lowest is 3. Carlos: colors between 4 and 9, lowest is 4. Dana: colors between 4 and 7, lowest is 4. Alex is winning right now despite having no color above 8, purely because of balance. That is the beauty of this game.

walkthrough.title: Going for Ingenious
walkthrough.text: Carlos spots an opportunity. His yellow is at 14 and there is a long line of yellow on the board. He places a tile that scores 5 more yellow, pushing him to 19, but wait, the cap is 18. His peg stops at 18 and he calls out Ingenious! He earns a bonus play. Any points beyond 18 are lost, but the bonus play itself is very valuable. Carlos plays a second tile, this time targeting his weakest color.

walkthrough.title: Using the Bonus Play Wisely
walkthrough.text: Carlos uses his bonus play to shore up his weakest color rather than pushing another color to 18. This is almost always the right call. The bonus play is like a free extra turn. Use it to fix your lowest score, not to pad an already-high color. Carlos scores 3 points in his weakest color, bringing his lowest from 4 to 7. That single bonus play raised his effective score by 3.

walkthrough.title: Mistake 3: Blocking Yourself
walkthrough.text: Dana places a tile that cuts off a line she was planning to extend next turn. She put a non-matching color tile right in the path of a promising purple line. Now the line is broken and she cannot score big from it anymore. Before placing a tile, always think about what lines you are building and what placements might block your own future scoring opportunities.

walkthrough.title: Blocking Opponents
walkthrough.text: Alex notices that Beth has a long line of red building up, and Beth's red is already high. Alex has a tile with a non-red hexagon and deliberately places it in Beth's path, breaking her red line. This is a legal and strategic move. While you should focus primarily on your own scoring, disrupting an opponent's long line can be worthwhile, especially if it does not cost you much.

walkthrough.title: The Board Gets Tight
walkthrough.text: As the game progresses, empty spaces become scarce. Players now have fewer options for tile placement. This is where the game gets really interesting. You might be forced to place tiles in spots that only score you 0 or 1 point. Tile placement becomes a puzzle: how do you squeeze the most value out of the remaining spaces while still feeding your weakest color?

walkthrough.title: Late-Game Priorities
walkthrough.text: With only a few turns left, everyone focuses laser-sharp on their lowest color. Alex's lowest is blue at 7. Beth's lowest is purple at 6. Carlos's lowest is orange at 7. Dana's lowest is green at 5. Dana is in trouble. She desperately needs green tiles and green-friendly board positions. Every tile placement now revolves around that weakest color.

walkthrough.title: The Final Turns
walkthrough.text: The last few tiles are placed. The board is full and no more legal placements exist. Time to count up. Alex's lowest color is blue at 8. Beth's lowest is purple at 7. Carlos's lowest is orange at 8. Dana's lowest is green at 6. Alex and Carlos are tied with a lowest of 8.

walkthrough.title: Resolving the Tie
walkthrough.text: Alex and Carlos both have a lowest color at 8. We go to the tiebreaker: compare the next-lowest color. Alex's next lowest is 9. Carlos's next lowest is 8. Alex wins the tiebreaker and takes the game. Beth finishes third with 7 and Dana finishes fourth with 6. Everyone shakes hands and immediately wants to play again.

walkthrough.title: Key Takeaways from This Game
walkthrough.text: Let's recap the big lessons from this practice game. First, balance is everything. Alex won by keeping all six colors close together, not by maxing out any single color. Second, the tile swap is your safety net when you are stuck without your weakest color. Third, the Ingenious bonus play is powerful but only if you use it to shore up weaknesses. And finally, watch the board for blocking opportunities, both to protect your lines and to disrupt opponents.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Play!
walkthrough.text: You now know everything you need to play Ingenious. Place tiles, score both hexagons, balance all six colors, and remember that your weakest color is your score. The game is simple to learn but the strategy is wonderfully deep. Go ahead and start a real game. You are going to do great!

summary.title: Key Takeaways from This Game

Use tile swaps and Ingenious bonus plays to fix weaknesses

summary.title: Ready to Play!
bullets: You know the rules, you have seen common mistakes, and you are ready
Place tiles, score both hexagons, keep all colors balanced — go have fun!

walkthrough.title: Always Check Your Lowest Color
walkthrough.text: Before every tile placement, glance at your score board and identify your lowest color. Ask yourself: can this tile help that color? If it can, it is almost certainly the right play. Your score is only as good as your weakest color, so feeding the bottom should be your default priority on every single turn.

walkthrough.title: Score Both Hexagons Carefully
walkthrough.text: Take your time when scoring. Check all five directions from each hexagon on your placed tile. It is easy to miss a diagonal line, especially when the board gets crowded. Count slowly and deliberately. A missed point early in the game can be the difference between winning and losing at the end.

walkthrough.title: Use the Tile Swap When You Can
walkthrough.text: The tile swap is one of the most powerful tools in the game, and many new players forget it exists. If your rack does not contain your lowest color, take advantage of the swap. A fresh set of six tiles gives you many more options. Do not hold onto a bad hand out of stubbornness.

walkthrough.title: Think One Turn Ahead
walkthrough.text: Before you place a tile, think about what your opponents might do on their turns. Are you leaving a juicy line open for the next player? Could your placement set up a great spot for you next turn? Thinking just one move ahead gives you a huge advantage over players who are only looking at the current turn.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Chase Ingenious Too Early
walkthrough.text: Getting a color to 18 and calling Ingenious feels amazing, and the bonus play is genuinely valuable. But do not sacrifice your other colors to rush one color to the top. An Ingenious in yellow does not help if your purple is stuck at 2. The bonus play is most powerful when your other colors are already in decent shape.

walkthrough.title: Pay Attention to Board Geography
walkthrough.text: The hexagonal board creates natural corridors and intersections. Placing tiles at spots where multiple lines converge gives you the biggest scoring potential. Look for spots where your tile can extend lines in two or three directions simultaneously. These junction points are the most valuable real estate on the board.

walkthrough.title: Watch What Opponents Need
walkthrough.text: Keep an eye on your opponents' score boards. If someone has a color at 1 and you can see them building a line of that color, you might want to block that line with a non-matching tile. Defensive play is just as important as offensive play, especially in a tight game with experienced players.

walkthrough.title: Stay Flexible with Your Tiles
walkthrough.text: Do not get locked into a plan and ignore what the board is telling you. Maybe you drew all red tiles but there are no good red spots available. Play the best tile for the current board state, even if it is not the color you wanted to play. Flexibility and adaptability win more games than rigid planning.

summary.title: Always Check Your Lowest Color
bullets: Identify your lowest color before every placement
Feeding your weakest color should be the default priority

summary.title: Score Both Hexagons Carefully
bullets: Check all 5 directions from each hexagon on your placed tile
Take your time — missed points add up

summary.title: Use the Tile Swap When You Can
bullets: Swap your hand if it has none of your lowest color
A fresh hand of 6 tiles is a powerful reset

summary.title: Think One Turn Ahead
bullets: Consider what opponents might do with the spots you leave open
Set up future scoring for yourself when possible

summary.title: Do Not Chase Ingenious Too Early
bullets: Maxing one color at the cost of neglecting others is a trap
The bonus play is best when your other colors are already solid

summary.title: Pay Attention to Board Geography
bullets: Hex intersections where multiple lines converge are the most valuable spots
Placing at junctions maximizes scoring potential

summary.title: Watch What Opponents Need
bullets: Check opponents' score boards for their weak and strong colors
Block their promising lines when it does not cost you much

summary.title: Stay Flexible with Your Tiles
bullets: Adapt to the board state — do not force a plan
Play the best tile for the situation, not the color you wanted

walkthrough.title: The Equalization Principle
walkthrough.text: Advanced players live by one rule: always raise the floor. Your effective score is your lowest color, so every decision should be filtered through the question: does this raise my minimum? A move that pushes your lowest from 6 to 8 is far more valuable than one that pushes a mid-tier color from 10 to 14. Think of your six colors as a chain. The chain is only as strong as its weakest link.

walkthrough.title: Tile Valuation
walkthrough.text: Not all tiles are created equal. A double tile, one with the same color on both hexagons, is extremely powerful for that specific color because you get to score that color twice from a single placement. Mixed tiles are more flexible but less impactful. Learn to recognize which tiles in your hand are your workhorses and which are fillers. Save your best tiles for moments when the board offers a high-scoring position.

walkthrough.title: Line Building vs. Line Joining
walkthrough.text: There are two ways to score big. Line building means extending a line by adding matching tiles to one end. Line joining means placing a tile between two existing groups of the same color, connecting them into one long line. Line joining is incredibly powerful because you suddenly count all symbols from both groups. Watch for opportunities to bridge two separate clusters of the same color.

walkthrough.title: Defensive Placement
walkthrough.text: As you improve, you will start reading the board for what opponents are building. If you see someone constructing a long line of their weakest color, consider whether you can block it by placing a non-matching tile in their path. The best defensive plays are ones that also score well for you. A pure blocking move that scores you nothing is rarely worth it. Look for plays that accomplish both goals simultaneously.

walkthrough.title: Timing Your Ingenious
walkthrough.text: Reaching 18 in a color and earning a bonus play is strategically rich. The best time to hit Ingenious is when you have a tile in hand that perfectly addresses your weakest color. That way, your bonus play directly raises your floor score. Hitting Ingenious when your hand is full of the wrong colors wastes the bonus. Time your Ingenious pushes so the bonus play has maximum impact.

walkthrough.title: Controlling Board Space
walkthrough.text: The board is finite and space gets tight. Experienced players think about territorial control: which regions of the board are dominated by which colors. If you can establish a cluster of your weak color in an area with room to grow, you have future scoring potential. If that area gets walled off by opponents, those scoring opportunities vanish. Claim space early for colors you know you will need later.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Bag
walkthrough.text: There are 120 tiles in the game, and each color combination appears a certain number of times. As the game progresses, you can get a rough sense of what is left in the bag based on what has been played on the board and what is on your rack. If you see a lot of blue already on the board, there is less blue left to draw. This informs whether the tile swap is likely to help you and which colors your opponents might struggle to find.

walkthrough.title: The Swap as a Strategic Tool
walkthrough.text: New players view the tile swap as an emergency measure. Advanced players sometimes engineer situations where a swap is beneficial. If you have played all the useful tiles from your rack and your hand is full of colors you do not need, deliberately playing tiles that leave your weakest color absent from your rack can trigger a legal swap. This is especially powerful late in the game when your remaining tiles are all wrong for your needs.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Calculation
walkthrough.text: In the final few turns, stop thinking about the big picture and start counting. How many tiles can you still play? What is the maximum you could score in your weakest color with those remaining placements? Is it mathematically possible to catch the leader? Sometimes the answer is yes, and you need to find the optimal sequence. Sometimes the answer is no, and you should shift to securing your current position against other trailing players.

walkthrough.title: Partnership Meta-Strategy
walkthrough.text: In the 4-player partnership variant, advanced teams learn to read their partner's intentions without explicit communication. If your partner keeps placing blue tiles, they probably need blue. You can help by not blocking blue lines in shared areas of the board. The best partnerships develop an intuitive understanding of each other's weaknesses and coordinate implicitly through their tile placements.

summary.title: The Equalization Principle
bullets: Always raise the floor — your lowest color IS your score
A move raising your minimum from 6 to 8 beats raising a mid-tier from 10 to 14

summary.title: Tile Valuation
bullets: Double-color tiles score one color twice — very powerful for that color
Save strong tiles for high-scoring board positions

summary.title: Line Building vs. Line Joining
bullets: Line building: extend a line from one end
Line joining: bridge two separate groups — counts all symbols from both sides

summary.title: Defensive Placement
bullets: Block opponents' long lines of their weak color
Best blocks also score well for you — pure blocks are rarely worth it

summary.title: Timing Your Ingenious
bullets: Hit 18 when your hand has a tile ready to fix your weakest color
Wasted bonus plays happen when your hand is wrong for your needs

summary.title: Controlling Board Space
bullets: Establish clusters of your weak colors in areas with room to grow
Opponents can wall off areas, cutting off future scoring potential

summary.title: Reading the Bag
bullets: Track what colors are heavy on the board — less of those remain in the bag
Informs swap decisions and predictions about opponents' hands

summary.title: The Swap as a Strategic Tool
bullets: Can be engineered, not just used in emergencies
Play out tiles of unneeded colors to trigger a legal swap

summary.title: Endgame Calculation
bullets: Count remaining placements and maximum possible improvement
Calculate whether catching the leader is feasible — or secure current position

summary.title: Partnership Meta-Strategy
bullets: Read your partner's plays — repeated color placements signal their needs
Avoid blocking colors your partner appears to be building

entries.definition: A domino-shaped piece made of two connected hexagons, each bearing a color symbol. Tiles are drawn from the bag and placed on the board to score points. There are 120 tiles total in the game.
A single six-sided space on the board. Each tile covers exactly two connected hexagons. Each hexagon on a tile shows one color symbol.
The central hexagonal board covered in hex spaces. Board size scales with player count: white spaces for 2 players, add light grey for 3, use the entire board for 4 players.
The cloth bag that holds all 120 tiles. Players draw tiles from the bag to refill their rack. Tiles returned during a swap also go back into the bag.
A stand that holds a player's 6 tiles upright and hidden from other players, similar to a Scrabble rack. Placed in front of the score board for privacy.
A personal board with six colored tracks running from 0 to 18. Each track corresponds to one of the six color symbols. Players advance pegs along these tracks as they score.
A small peg placed on the score board to track a player's progress in one color. Each player uses six pegs, one per color. Pegs start at 0 and advance as the player scores.

One of six color symbols pre-printed directly on the game board near the center. These serve as starting anchors for the first round and count as matching symbols for scoring purposes throughout the game.
A tile where both hexagons show the same color symbol. Doubles score the same color from both hexagons, making them very powerful for advancing a single color quickly.
A tile where each hexagon shows a different color symbol. Mixed tiles are more versatile because they can advance two different colors in a single placement.

A straight row of hexagons following one of the six directions on the hex grid. Scoring counts consecutive matching symbols along each of the five lines radiating from a placed hexagon (excluding the direction covered by the other half of the tile).

The process of counting matching symbols along the five outward lines from each hexagon on a newly placed tile. Each hexagon is scored separately, and matching pegs on the score board are advanced accordingly.

When scoring, you count symbols in a line that match your placed hexagon's color. Counting stops at the first empty space or different-colored symbol. The symbol on the tile you just placed is never counted.
The maximum value any single color peg can reach on the score board. Points that would push a peg beyond 18 are lost. Reaching 18 triggers the Ingenious bonus.

The call made when a player's color peg reaches the maximum value of 18. The player earns an immediate bonus play of one additional tile before drawing to refill their rack. Multiple Ingenious calls can chain in a single turn.

A free extra tile placement earned by reaching 18 in any color. The player places and scores another tile from their rack before drawing. If the bonus tile triggers another Ingenious, another bonus play is earned.
The color with the lowest peg position on a player's score board. This is the player's final score. The entire game revolves around maximizing this value across all six colors.

A special action available when a player's rack contains none of their lowest-scoring color. The player shows their tiles to all opponents, sets them aside, draws 6 new tiles, then returns the old tiles to the bag. Performed before the normal end-of-turn draw.

A special rule for the opening round: each player must place their first tile adjacent to a different one of the six pre-printed symbols on the board. After the first round, tiles can go on any two connected empty spaces.
The game ends when no further tiles can be legally placed on the board. At that point, each player's lowest color peg determines their score. The player with the highest lowest score wins.
When players are tied on their lowest color, the next-lowest color is compared. If still tied, continue comparing progressively higher colors until the tie is resolved.
If a player manages to advance all six color pegs to 18, they win immediately regardless of the current game state. This is extremely rare but ends the game on the spot.

A 4-player variant where players sitting opposite each other form a team. Partners share a double-length score board (two boards placed end to end) and both contribute scores to the shared tracks. Communication rules are agreed upon before the game.
Two regular score boards placed end to end, used in the partnership and solitaire variants. Allows scores to exceed 18 on the first board by continuing onto the second board at value 0.

A solo variant using only the white spaces of the board and a double-length score board. No rack is used. The player draws one tile at a time, places and scores it, then draws the next. No bonus plays are awarded. The goal is to achieve the highest possible lowest score.

The game board scales to player count. 2 players use only white spaces for a tighter, more competitive experience. 3 players add light grey spaces. 4 players use the entire board including the outer ring.
The core strategic concept of Ingenious: keeping all six colors as close together as possible rather than maximizing any single color. Since your score equals your lowest, balance is paramount.

A scoring technique where a tile is placed between two existing groups of the same color, connecting them. The placed hexagon then counts all matching symbols from both groups, resulting in a large score from a single placement.

Deliberately placing a tile with a non-matching color in the path of an opponent's line to prevent them from extending it for a large score. Most effective when the blocking placement also scores well for the blocker.
A strategic term for a player's current lowest color value. Raising the floor score is the primary objective of every placement decision in competitive play.
